actions.thought: Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium holding in a three-handed game, especially from the button position. I want to build a pot and maintain initiative against the small and big blinds. A raise to 250 chips (2.5x the big blind) is a standard opening size that helps me define my range and capitalize on my positional advantage.

I am holding 94 offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand to defend against an open raise from the Button, especially while playing out of position. To conserve my stack and avoid unnecessary losses early in the tournament, I will fold.

Holding Ace-King suited in the Big Blind facing a Button open, a 3-bet is mandatory for value and to seize the initiative out of position. I will 3-bet to roughly 3.5x the original raise (900) to build the pot and price out marginal hands while giving us a favorable stack-to-pot ratio going to the flop.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, calling is the most balanced approach with Ace-Jack offsuit. While AJo is a strong hand, 4-betting this early in the tournament against an unknown range is unnecessarily high variance. By calling, I maintain a manageable pot size and leverage my position to play post-flop, where I can reassess based on the board texture.

We missed the flop completely on a Qc 7d 4c board. Our AdKd gives us two overcards and a backdoor flush draw, but we are out of position against a Button calling range that contains plenty of Qx (AQ, KQ, QJs), pocket pairs (88-JJ), and club flush draws. A continuation bet here would often get called or raised, putting us in a difficult spot. Checking to pot control and evaluating the opponent's action is the most prudent play. We can easily fold to a significant bet given our lack of equity and position.

The flop is Qc 7d 4c. My opponent, who was the pre-flop 3-bettor, checked to me. This is a common spot to exercise pot control with Ace-Jack high. While I have the Ace of clubs for a backdoor flush draw, checking back is the most strategic move to preserve my stack, realize my equity, and see a turn card for free, especially since the Queen on the board is likely to hit my opponent's 3-betting range.

The 9d on the turn is an excellent card for us, giving us the nut flush draw to go along with our two overcards. Since Flashlight checked back the flop, their range is somewhat capped, likely consisting of marginal pairs like 88-JJ, weak Qx, or missed draws. Betting now serves as a high-equity semi-bluff. It puts pressure on their middle pairs and takes the initiative, while also building the pot for the times we hit our flush or an Ace/King on the river. I'll size my bet around half-pot to generate fold equity while keeping the risk reasonable.

I am facing a bet of 900 into a pot of 1850 on a turn board of Qc 7d 4c 9d. With the Ace of clubs, I hold the nut flush draw, which provides significant equity against a wide range of hands. Pot odds dictate that a call is mathematically sound, as the price is roughly 25% of the total pot and I have substantial outs to improve to the best hand on the river. Raising would be overly aggressive against an unknown range, while folding would be an overreaction and surrender significant equity.

We missed all our draws and overcards on the river, leaving us with just Ace-King high. The opponent's line of checking the flop and calling the turn bet strongly points toward a made hand with showdown value, such as a weak Queen, pocket pair (like JJ), or a middle pair that just improved (like JTs or T9s). Attempting to bluff this river would require a substantial sizing and relies heavily on folding out sticky one-pair hands, which is a low-probability play on a board where many straight and two-pair combinations just completed. Checking to surrender is the most prudent decision, as it preserves our stack, and we occasionally win against missed flush draws that check behind.

I have reached the river with Ace-high, having missed my flush draw and straight draw. My opponent (Pro) checked to me, indicating a likely surrender or a trapped range. Betting here would likely only be called by a pair or better, turning my hand into a bluff that is unlikely to succeed against the range Pro 3-bet pre-flop. Given that I have some showdown value with Ace-high, checking behind is the most prudent decision to see if it holds up, rather than risking more chips with a bluff.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. While being on the button allows for a wide opening range in a 3-handed game, J2o is near the bottom of all starting hands and lacks both post-flop playability and high-card strength. With the tournament just beginning and stacks being relatively deep, there is no need to play such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
Pocket Tens is a premium starting hand, especially in a blind vs blind situation. Raising to 3 big blinds to build the pot and extract value, as well as take control of the hand going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 3d, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability. Calling 200 into a pot of 400 is not mathematically profitable given the low equity of this hand against a standard Small Blind opening range. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.
K5 offsuit is a weak hand. Even being the first to act on the button in 3-handed play, it is generally best to fold this and wait for a stronger holding to steal the blinds or play a pot.

Holding King-Three offsuit in the small blind is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against the big blind. Since the button has already folded, I have the option to complete the bet or fold. Given the lack of strength and the disadvantage of playing out of position post-flop, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to conserve my stack in the early stages of the tournament.

6-5 offsuit is a weak, non-connected hand with poor playability, especially in an unraised pot. Opening this from the button is suboptimal as it will often be dominated or put in a tough spot on the flop. Preserving my stack for better opportunities in this tournament is the correct strategic approach.

In a blind vs blind situation after the button folds, T8o is a reasonable hand to complete the small blind. I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop against the Big Blind. While raising is a valid strategy to steal the blinds, calling allows me to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has some connectivity and high-card value in a wide-range scenario.

With Ten-Three offsuit, I hold a very weak hand. Since the Small Blind only limped and I am in the Big Blind with no additional chips to call, I should just check and take the free flop. Raising as a bluff with such marginal cards is unnecessary risk at this stage.

The flop is Kh-Kd-9h. I have Ten-high with no direct draw. In a limped pot against the Big Blind, checking is the most prudent play. The paired board is likely to hit neither player hard, but I have no reason to build a pot with air. I will check and see how the opponent reacts.

With ten-high and a paired board of Kings, I have virtually no equity. Flash checked the flop, and since there is no reason to invest chips into a bluff with such a weak hand, checking is the standard and safest play.

The turn 7s doesn't significantly change the board texture. Having checked the flop with Ten-high and no real draw, I have very little showdown value. However, in a limped pot where both players checked the flop, the ranges are extremely wide. I will check again to see if Pro checks back, which allows me a free river card to potentially hit a Ten or an Eight, or to potentially bluff if the river is favorable. Betting here is unlikely to fold out any pair or King, and checking keeps the pot small.

With ten-high and no draws on this board, my hand has virtually zero showdown value. Since Flash checked again on the turn, there is no need to unnecessarily risk chips on a bluff. Taking a free card to the river is the safest and most optimal play.

The board is Kh-Kd-9h-7s-6d and the pot is limped with both players checking every street so far. I have Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, in a tiny pot of 200 chips, there is little incentive to bluff as even a small pair or Ace-high might call. I will check and hope for a check-back to see if my Ten-high can somehow win against a lower air hand, though I expect to lose this small pot most of the time.

With ten-high on a board of K-K-9-7-6, my hand has virtually no showdown value. However, the pot is very small (200) and Flash has shown no interest by checking all three streets. While a bluff might occasionally fold out Ace-high or Queen-high, it's not worth risking chips when my opponent could easily be trapping with a weak pair or call with better high cards. Checking behind is the safest decision.
K9o on the Button in a 3-handed game is a strong enough hand to open for value and to put pressure on the blinds. A standard raise size of 3x BB is appropriate here to take control of the hand.

Facing a 3BB open from the button, A6o in the Small Blind is too weak to call due to positional disadvantage and reverse implied odds. 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessarily risky at this stage. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play.

With AdJh, I have a strong hand against a button open, and I am in the Big Blind. 3-betting allows me to take control of the hand and gain initiative. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing for a 3-bet out of position, which provides value and denies equity to the opponent's wider opening range.

I opened K9o from the button and faced a 3-bet to 1000 from Flashlight in the Big Blind. While I have position, K9o is a relatively weak hand to call a 3-bet with, as it is often dominated by the 3-betting ranges of most players (like AK, AJ, KQ, or KJ). Calling 700 more into a pot of 1350 is a borderline decision mathematically, but post-flop playability is poor. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to avoid significant chip loss early in the tournament.
K2o is a very weak hand to play even from the button. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve chips for better starting hands.

With Ace-Six offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. Ace-high is a strong enough hand in this scenario to open the pot and take the initiative. Raising allows me to apply pressure and potentially take down the pot pre-flop, which is a standard strategic move when the button folds in a shorthanded game.

I have J8o in the big blind against a small blind raise from Flashlight. The pot odds are favorable (150 to call into a 350 pot), and I will have position throughout the hand. While J8o is a marginal hand, in a 3-handed game, it is strong enough to defend the big blind against a standard steal attempt from the small blind. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my hand's strength in position.

The flop is low and disconnected (3-7-4), which is unlikely to have hit the big blind's calling range significantly. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet to represent strength and potentially take down the pot immediately. Ace-high is a vulnerable hand, but it still has some showdown value and equity if called, so a standard continuation bet of about half-pot is the most strategic approach to control the betting.

On a dry flop of 3h-7c-4s, my hand (J8o) has missed completely. Flashlight's lead of 250 into 500 is a standard continuation bet. While I have two overcards, they are relatively weak, and I have no significant draws. Calling here would essentially be a float with very low equity and limited ways to improve. Given the lack of connection with the board, folding is the most disciplined and chip-preserving play.
Ten-seven offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, even in a three-handed game on the button. Folding preserves my stack to wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. While Flashlight folded on the button, T3o is a very weak hand to play even in a blind-vs-blind situation. Playing out of position against 'Pro' with such a weak hand is likely to lead to losses. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jh 3h. While suited, J3 is a weak hand even for a 3-handed button opening range. Looking at previous hands, Flashlight has shown willingness to 3-bet (Hand #5), and Pro has also been active. Folding here is the most prudent strategy to preserve my 95 BB stack and wait for a hand with better playability and equity realized post-flop.

Holding 92 offsuit in the small blind, this hand is extremely weak and completely unplayable, especially since I'll be out of position against the Big Blind post-flop. The correct and standard decision here is to fold.
K7o is a weak unsuited hand. Even from the Button, opening with it is marginal at best. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand, preserving my stack.

4d Jh is a weak, disconnected hand that does not warrant a call or a raise from the small blind position, even heads-up against the big blind. Given the goal to maximize stack size over 150 hands, folding is the most profitable long-term strategy here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a standard opening hand on the button, especially when attempting to steal the blinds. Raising allows me to take the initiative and utilize my position throughout the hand. 300 chips (3x the big blind) is a standard open sizing to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o. Flashlight raised to 3x from the Button. K3o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially with a chip leader like Pro still to act in the Big Blind. The hand lacks post-flop playability and calling here would often lead to difficult situations where I am behind. Folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play in this spot to preserve my stack.

7c8d offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x Button raise. While the pot odds are okay, playing out of position post-flop with an unsuited, middling connector is unprofitable. Folding is the most straightforward and solid decision.
K9o is a strong enough hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. Raising to 3x BB puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the pot early. My stack is healthy at 95bb.
I have 4c 8d in the small blind, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the button, folding is the only viable option.

The pot odds are very favorable, requiring me to call 200 to win a total pot of 650. Q5 suited is a reasonable hand to defend from the big blind against a button raise, so I will call to see the flop.

I have bottom pair on a coordinated board with potential flush and straight draws. Being out of position, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on my opponent's action. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor with a marginal hand like this would be unwise.

The flop of 8h-5s-6s is highly coordinated and likely connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (suited connectors, small pairs, and straight draws). While I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7), this board is dangerous to c-bet into because Flashlight is unlikely to fold many hands. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and potentially hit my draw or improve to top pair on the turn without facing a check-raise.

With bottom pair and a draw-heavy board, my hand is a marginal bluff-catcher. As the out-of-position player, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot and evaluate the opponent's action on the turn. Betting into the opponent would likely result in me being called by better hands or facing a difficult raise.

On the turn, the Jack of Hearts is an overcard that doesn't significantly help my King-high hand, though I still have a gutshot straight draw. Flashlight has checked twice now, indicating either a weak hand or a desire to get to showdown cheaply. While a bet might force out some of his weaker holdings, checking back again allows me to realize my equity for free. I have some showdown value against total air and don't want to risk being check-raised off my draw on such a coordinated board. I will check and look to improve on the river or potentially win at showdown if the board stays relatively safe.

The board is 8h 5s 6s Jh Th, and I hold 5d Qd for a pair of fives. The hand has been checked down by both players on the flop and turn. My pair is very weak, likely only good against pure air or small overcards that the opponent gave up with. Betting would be turning my marginal showdown value into a bluff, which is unlikely to succeed against a standard button opening range that checked back. Checking allows me to reach showdown as cheaply as possible against the opponent's missed hands.

The board has become quite coordinated with 8-5-6-J-T, and while I have a King-high, it has almost no showdown value against Flashlight's Big Blind defending range, which likely contains many small pairs or even Ace-high hands that currently beat me. However, Flashlight has checked for the third time, indicating significant weakness. While a bluff might occasionally work, the board texture (specifically the straight and two-pair possibilities) makes it difficult to represent a strong hand after I checked the flop and turn. Checking behind is the most prudent play to minimize losses, as I am unlikely to fold out better hands on this specific board without a significant, risky overbet.
We are 3-handed on the Button with JTo. This is a very standard open raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the SB or BB. I will raise to 300 (3 big blinds).

I am in the small blind with 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Calling a raise out of position with such a disconnected, low-equity hand would be a negative expectation play. I need to maintain discipline and fold, waiting for a better opportunity to contest the pot.

I have been dealt pocket kings (Kc Ks), which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Pro raised to 300 from the button, and with Flashlight folding, it is a head-up situation. I must 3-bet here to build value and protect my hand against the button's wide opening range. Raising to 900 is a standard sizing to put pressure on Pro and maximize the pot size.

We opened JTo from the Button aiming to steal the blinds, but the Big Blind 3-bet to 900. Offsuit broadway hands like JTo do not perform well against a 3-bet range, as they are often dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, or large pocket pairs. Without a suited advantage, calling here is mathematically unprofitable. Folding is the correct move.

I am holding King-Three offsuit on the button. This is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game, especially facing a standard big blind requirement of 100 chips. There is no strategic value in calling here, as the hand is easily dominated and has poor post-flop potential. I will fold to conserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J8 offsuit. Since the button folded, it's just me and the Big Blind (Pro). J8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Although it only costs 50 chips to call, I risk being raised by a larger stack (Pro) or playing a difficult post-flop game. In a long tournament format, I prefer to fold these mediocre hands from the small blind to avoid unnecessary losses against aggressive opponents.

K8o is a strong enough hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. Raising to 3x blinds is standard to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a controlled pot in position.

I am dealt Queen-Seven offsuit (Qc 7d) in the Small Blind. The Button has opened with a 3x raise. Q7o is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand that plays very poorly out of position, especially facing a raise. The correct strategic decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Five offsuit, which is a weak hand that does not play well out of position. The button has opened, and while the pot odds are tempting, calling with such a marginal hand is generally negative expected value given the disadvantage of playing the rest of the hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Holding AQo on the Button, this is a premium starting hand and a clear open-raise. I will make it 3x the big blind to 300 to build the pot, take the initiative, and put pressure on the blinds.

K2 offsuit is a weak hand, particularly from the Small Blind out of position against a Button raise. With 90BB stacks, there is no need to defend wide with trash hands that are difficult to play post-flop. Folding is the disciplined and correct long-term play.

I am in the big blind with 6h 3h. Pro raised from the button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), 63 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against the chip leader. In a 150-hand tournament, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with the bottom of my range. I will fold and preserve my stack.

With K8 offsuit in the button position, the hand is marginal and lacks sufficient strength to play profitably against two opponents. While stealing the blinds is a valid strategy in 3-handed play, K8o is at the bottom of a standard opening range, and folding is a disciplined way to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 92s. Flashlight has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). While 92s is suited, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against an opponent like Pro who has a larger stack and has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding here is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8c 6s, which is a weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. While I have the advantage of position, both opponents have shown willingness to defend their blinds or 3-bet (specifically Flashlight in Hand #5). Given the mediocre strength of my hand and the stack depths, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking chips on a low-equity steal attempt.
Holding K3 offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds. This hand is too weak to play out of position against the Big Blind, even for a steal attempt. Folding is the standard and safest play.
Ten-Deuce suited is a very weak hand, well outside of standard opening ranges even from the Button in a 3-handed game. The optimal play is to fold and wait for better starting hands to commit chips.

With a very weak hand (2s 8h) in the small blind and the button having folded, the most disciplined play is to fold rather than investing more chips to see a flop out of position. This preserves my stack for better opportunities.

KdJh is a strong starting hand on the button. Raising is preferred to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure.

I have Ace-Jack of diamonds in the Small Blind. This is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has raised from the button, which is typically a wide range. I will 3-bet (raise) for value and to take initiative in the hand, as calling puts me out of position for the rest of the hand without control of the pot. A raise to 1,000 is a standard size to apply pressure and define the range of my opponents.
I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3s, which is a very weak hand. Facing an initial raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, folding is the only viable option.

Flash has 3-bet from the small blind. With KdJh in position, I have a solid hand that can play well post-flop. A 4-bet here would be risky as it might isolate me against a range that dominates mine (like AA, KK, QQ, or AK). Calling allows me to control the pot size while retaining the advantage of position, which is crucial for navigating the post-flop play.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Small Blind. The flop comes 5s-5d-6s, which is a paired board that generally favors my range over the caller's, as it is difficult for the Button to have many 5s in their 3-bet calling range. I have Ace-high with two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. I will place a standard continuation bet of 700 (one-third of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong hand like an overpair, and force folds from Flashlight's non-paired broadway cards.

The opponent has bet 700 into a pot of 2800, providing 4:1 pot odds. While my King-Jack high is currently behind on a 5-5-6 board, the bet size is small and my two overcards provide decent equity against many of the opponent's holdings. Calling is mathematically justified to see the turn, where I can re-evaluate based on the next card or potentially take a bluff line if the board remains passive.

The deuce of clubs on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. After my flop continuation bet was called, I'll check here to control the pot size. My Ace-high still has showdown value against the opponent's potential missed draws or weaker overcards, and checking allows me to evaluate their response without committing more chips into a range that likely includes pocket pairs or a six.

Flash checked the turn on a 5-5-6-2 board. My King-high hand has minimal showdown value, and there is a significant risk that Flash is trapping with a 5 or a pocket pair, or simply checking to re-evaluate. Betting into this range would turn my hand into a bluff, which is inefficient given the board texture. Checking behind is the standard play in position to control the pot size and realize equity if I happen to improve on the river.

The river Jack provides me with top pair, top kicker on a board that paired fives. After checking the turn and seeing my opponent check back, it's unlikely they have a powerhouse like a five or a full house. I will place a value bet of 1800, which is roughly half the pot, to extract chips from medium pocket pairs like 77 through TT that my opponent likely holds after calling the pre-flop 3-bet and the flop continuation bet.

Flash's betting line (betting the flop, checking the turn, and betting the river) is often indicative of a polarized range, potentially representing a missed draw (such as spades or straight draws) that is turning into a bluff. My pair of Jacks, which I hit on the river, is a strong hand against these bluffs. While I could be behind a 5 or a pocket pair, the pot odds (calling 1800 to win a total pot of 5200) make this a clear call. My hand has sufficient showdown value to justify calling rather than folding.

I am on the button with King-Seven suited (Kh 7h), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Opening for a standard raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with initiative.

Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, King-Two offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the Small Blind. It has terrible equity, bad playability, and is easily dominated. Folding is the optimal choice.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Three offsuit, a weak hand that does not play well out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash. While the pot odds offer a call, the risk of playing a weak hand without initiative against a wider range on the button is not worth the potential chip loss. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger holding to contest the pot.

With pocket 4s on the Button in a 3-handed game, opening the pot with a raise is standard strategy. It allows me to steal the blinds or play in position with the betting initiative. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is consistent with my previous sizing and applies appropriate pressure.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The button (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a standard opening size. While A8o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend, especially in a three-handed game. Folding would be too conservative, and while 3-betting is an option to try and take the pot down pre-flop, playing a bloated pot out of position with this hand is risky given my stack size. I will call the 250 chips to see a flop and evaluate my position thereafter.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 6d. The Button (Pro) raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 900 total. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1, which is more than enough to justify calling with a hand like 76 offsuit to see a flop, especially since I close the action. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

The flop of Queen-Six-Deuce is very dry and did not connect with my Ace-Eight offsuit. Being out of position against two opponents, a continuation bet would be reckless and likely ineffective. My best course of action is to check, evaluate how my opponents respond to this board, and prepare to fold if significant aggression is shown, as my Ace-high is currently only a bluff-catcher.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a very dry board of Qd-6s-2h. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked to me. Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor on the button, I expect him to continuation bet frequently on this texture. I will check to see his action. Betting myself would likely only get called by better hands (Queens) or folds from hands I'm already beating. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot size and evaluate on the turn.

On a dry Q-6-2 rainbow board, my pocket 4s have marginal showdown value. Since this is a 3-way pot, a continuation bet is likely to be called only by better hands (like a Queen, a 6, or higher pocket pairs), while folding out hands I currently beat, such as Ace-high or King-high. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, keep weaker hands in their ranges, and try to realize my equity or hit a set on the turn.

The turn card, the nine of hearts, does not improve my Ace-high hand. Since the flop checked around, there is no reason to inflate the pot now with a marginal bluff-catcher. Betting would likely force folds from hands I am already beating, while hands that call would have me crushed. I will check again to maintain pot control and evaluate the river based on my opponents' actions.

I have second pair (sixes) on a board of Qd-6s-2h-9h. After the pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked back on the flop, it's likely that the strength of the table is relatively low. However, my sixes are still vulnerable and not strong enough to bet for value against two opponents. Flashlight has checked twice, and I will check behind to see a free river card and control the pot size. This keeps my options open for a cheap showdown or to evaluate based on the river action.

With pocket 4s on a board now containing multiple overcards (Queen, 9, 6) and facing two opponents, my hand is very vulnerable. A bet here is unlikely to fold out any hands that currently beat me, such as 9x, 6x, or higher pocket pairs, and I risk being called or raised by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind again keeps the pot small and allows me to see a free river card, giving me a chance to hit a two-outer or potentially get to a cheap showdown.

The river is the Jack of Clubs, which does not improve my Ace-high hand. Since the flop and turn were checked around by all three players, this has remained a small pot with no significant betting action. Betting now as a bluff is unlikely to work against two opponents, and my Ace-high hand has minimal showdown value. Checking is the most logical and conservative play to minimize potential losses and see if my Ace-high is the best hand at showdown for free.

I have a pair of sixes (6d 7c) on a board of Qd-6s-2h-9h-Jc. The action has been checked all the way through since the pre-flop raise. Flashlight (SB) has checked to me on the river. Since Pro (BTN), the original raiser, checked both the flop and turn, it is unlikely they have a strong hand like a Queen. However, with the Nine and Jack now on the board, my sixes are only the fourth-best pair possible. There is no value in betting here, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free, where my pair might still be the best hand against Ace-high or a smaller pocket pair.

On the river, the board contains four overcards to my pocket 4s (Queen, Jack, 9, 6). Since both opponents have checked through every street, their ranges are likely capped at marginal pairs or high-card hands like Ace-high or King-high. I actually beat Ace-high and King-high at showdown. If I bet, I am turning my hand into a bluff, and I risk getting called by any random pair that beat me, while only folding out hands I already beat. Checking to see a free showdown is the most sensible play here, as I still have a chance to win the pot against missed draws or high cards.

I am on the button with Queen of Clubs and Jack of Clubs. This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game, allowing me to take initiative and play in position. A standard open raise to 300 (3 big blinds) is an effective way to steal the blinds or build a pot with a premium suited connector.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise. While K9o is a semi-strong hand, it performs poorly out of position, especially against an open from the Button who likely has a wide range that includes many hands that dominate mine (like KJ, KQ, or any Ace). Given that I have a healthy stack and would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against the original raiser and potentially the Big Blind, the most disciplined professional play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while out of position in the Big Blind. Although J7s has some playability, it is a marginal hand that easily makes dominated pairs or weak draws. Calling a 3x raise out of position with this hand is unprofitable in the long run, and it's not a premium 3-bet bluff candidate. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. This is a marginal hand that is generally considered too weak to open, even in a three-handed game. While I have the chip lead and have been active in previous hands, J7o doesn't play well post-flop if called, and with both opponents having healthy enough stacks to defend, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to maintain my table image and wait for better starting requirements.

With King-Seven offsuit in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a marginal hand that will be out of position postflop against the big blind. While a steal attempt is an option, folding is the safest and most standard play to avoid getting involved in a tough spot out of position with a weak holding.

Holding J4s on the Button is a very weak starting hand, even in a 3-handed game. It doesn't have good playability or high card strength. Folding pre-flop is the most standard and EV-maximizing play here to avoid losing chips in marginal spots.

Pro (Button) has folded, leaving me in the Small Blind against Flash in the Big Blind. King-Jack offsuit is a strong holding in a blind-versus-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take initiative, potentially steal the blinds, or play a pot in position against a single opponent.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-5 offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 3x the blind. I need to call 200 more chips into a total pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Crucially, in this blind-vs-blind battle, I will have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind on a paired board (9-8-8). My hand (Ks, Jh) has no pair and only weak backdoor potential. This board texture hits the opponent's defending range well (many 8s and 9s). I will check to control the pot size and avoid inflating a pot without a made hand, keeping my range balanced.

I am in position on a paired board (9-8-8). My hand, 6-high, has very little showdown value but does have some back-door potential. Flashlight's check from the small blind could indicate weakness or a trap with an 8. Since I have 6-high and minimal equity, betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands like small pocket pairs or Ace-high. By checking back, I see a free turn card, which could give me a straight draw (if a 7 or 4 appears) and keeps the pot small while I'm behind.

I am currently in the Small Blind against the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. The board is 9s-8s-8c-Qc, which gives me a gutshot straight draw to a Ten. Both players checked the flop, indicating that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. Betting into a checked-down board with only King-high and a gutshot is risky, as it likely won't fold out any pair (like an 8 or 9) and offers poor risk-to-reward. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see the river for free to realize my straight draw equity.

Flashlight has checked twice on a 9-8-8-Q board after raising pre-flop. While this usually indicates weakness, my hand (6-high) has absolutely no equity or draws. Although a bet could potentially force a fold from hands like Ace-high or small pairs, I am currently the chip leader and there is no need to inflate the pot with a pure air hand. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and maintains pot control. I will re-evaluate on the river based on Flashlight's action.

I am at the river with King-high on a board with three 8s and a Queen. Given that the flop and turn were checked through, it is unlikely either of us has a monster, but my King-high is weak and will only beat total bluffs. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands (like a pair of 9s, Queens, or even an 8), and checking allows me to go to showdown without risking more chips on a thin bluff.

The board is 9s-8s-8c-Qc-8h, which provides a board-state full house (8-8-8-Q-9). My hand (6-5) does not improve upon this board. Any opponent holding an Ace, King, Jack, or Ten (all likely in a pre-flop raiser's range) would have a better five-card hand (e.g., 8-8-8-Q-A). While Flashlight has checked three times, suggesting weakness, even his 'weak' hands like Ace-high or King-high currently beat me. Since the pot is small and my hand has zero showdown value against his range, I will check back and likely lose or occasionally chop if he also holds very low cards.

K9o is a reasonably strong hand in a three-handed game when playing from the button. I want to seize the initiative pre-flop to potentially steal the blinds or define the hand range of my opponents. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 3x the big blind. While A2o has some raw equity against a standard button opening range, it plays very poorly out of position and is frequently dominated. Given the stack depths and the fact that Flashlight has shown a willingness to call 3-bets in previous hands (like Hand #1 and #19), I prefer a disciplined fold here to avoid a difficult post-flop spot out of position with a weak kicker.
Q6o is a weak offsuit hand with very little playability. Facing a 3x raise from the button, defending the big blind with this hand is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 7s 8c. This is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x) allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I will have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand. While 78o is not a premium hand, its connectivity provides some playability on various flop textures.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Three suited. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, J3s is a weak, disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind with 6d Th, which is a weak offsuit hand. While the pot odds are tempting, playing a marginal hand like 6-10 offsuit out of position against an aggressive button raiser is generally unprofitable. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.
J9o is a marginal hand even from the button. Without antes and facing competent blinds, opening this wide can easily be exploited. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

Ten-five suited is a weak, speculative hand to be playing out of position in the small blind. With the button folding, I have to commit an additional 50 chips to call the big blind. Given the stack depths and the marginal nature of my hand, it is more strategically sound to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable position or a stronger hand.

I am in the Button position with Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand that does not warrant an open-raise or a call in a 3-handed game, especially with two players still to act behind me. It is strategically sound to fold this marginal hand and preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. Since the Button (Flashlight) folded, the action is on me. In a blind vs blind situation, J6o is strong enough to call the extra 50 chips to see a flop, given the 3:1 pot odds. Raising is an option to steal, but calling allows me to see a flop cheaply while out of position against the Big Blind (Pro).

Flash limped from the Small Blind. Holding 98o in the Big Blind, this is a marginal hand that plays decently post-flop, but isn't quite strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff here. Checking and seeing a free flop in position is the standard and optimal play.

I have top pair on a relatively safe board in a blind vs blind pot. Pro checked pre-flop, suggesting they don't have a premium hand. I will lead with a value bet to protect against draws and get value from weaker hands like middle pair or straight draws.

I flopped middle pair on a coordinated Jack-high board with a flush draw present. Facing a half-pot probe bet from the Small Blind who limped pre-flop, my hand is definitely too strong to fold, as it beats many bluffs, straight draws, and smaller pairs in his wide range. However, it is not strong enough to raise for value, and turning it into a bluff makes little sense. Calling is the optimal line to keep his bluffs in the pot and exercise pot control while re-evaluating on the turn.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Jacks with a 6). The turn 7d opens up some straight draws and a diamond flush draw alongside the existing club draw. Since I led the flop and was called by Pro, I want to continue extracting value from draws and weaker pairs (9x, 4x, or small pocket pairs). Checking might invite a bluff, but betting 250 into a 400 pot maintains initiative and protects my hand against the numerous draws on this board.

Facing a second barrel on a very wet and coordinated turn card (J-9-7-4 with two flush draws). Our middle pair and weak kicker, along with a gutshot to a Ten, is a marginal hand that is likely behind a Jack, a better 9, or a stronger draw. We also lack a flush draw to back up our equity. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve our stack, avoiding a large pot against continued aggression.
I am on the button with QJs, a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure on the blinds.

Facing a standard 3x button open from Flash, pocket eights is a strong starting hand that plays well as a 3-bet from the Small Blind. By 3-betting, I aim to isolate the Button, deter the Big Blind from squeezing, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. It also helps define the strength of my hand and avoids playing a multi-way pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce suited. There is an open to 300 and a 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind. Calling here would be out of position in a bloated pot with a weak hand that does not play well in multi-way 3-bet pots. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I opened QJs from the button and faced a 3-bet from the Small Blind. QJs is a strong hand that plays exceptionally well post-flop, especially in position. The 3-bet size is standard. I have a significant stack advantage and calling allows me to use my positional advantage on the flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially stronger range. Folding would be too exploitable given the strength of my hand 3-handed.

I 3-bet pre-flop and the flop comes low with only one overcard (the 9) to my pocket eights. Holding the 8 of spades gives me minor backdoor potential and blocks some of my opponent's spade combinations. A continuation bet of about 1/3 pot (700) is standard here to deny equity to two-overcard hands, extract value from smaller pairs like 55-77 or straight/flush draws, and maintain the betting lead. This sizing pot-controls while adequately protecting my hand.

I have flopped a very strong draw with two overcards and a flush draw (QsJs on a 3s 9s 4h board). Pro's continuation bet of 700 into 2100 is a standard sizing for a 3-bet pot. Folding is out of the question given my equity. While raising is an option to apply pressure to hands like AK or AQ, calling is the most prudent play in position. It allows me to realize my equity, keeps Pro's bluffs in his range, and keeps the pot size manageable in case the turn doesn't improve my hand. If I hit my flush or an overcard, I am well-positioned to extract value on later streets.

The turn brings the 7 of spades, completing the flush draw and bringing in straight possibilities (like 56s). While holding the 8 of spades gives me a weak flush draw to go with my pair of eights, my hand's relative value decreases significantly on this wet, coordinated board. Betting here is likely to isolate myself against better hands such as completed flushes, bigger pairs, or higher spade draws. By checking, I control the size of the pot, keep my opponent's bluffing range wide, and maintain the option to evaluate their bet sizing or realize my equity if the turn checks through.

On the turn, the 7s completes my flush. Pro checked, which often indicates weakness or a pot-control line after being called on the flop in a 3-bet pot. A bet of around 40-50% of the pot (1400-1700) is appropriate to get called by hands like top pair, overpairs, or the Ace of spades. I will bet 1600.

The turn 7 of spades completes the flush and makes the board very coordinated. Facing a nearly half-pot bet from an opponent who called a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, my pair of eights is essentially reduced to a bluff-catcher. Although I hold the 8 of spades, if a fourth spade hits the river, my flush will frequently be dominated by higher spades (like As, Ks, Qs, Ts, Js) which are heavily present in their floating and semi-bluffing range. Calling out of position here will likely bleed chips against a range that contains better made hands or high-equity draws, so folding is the most disciplined play.

With 87 suited on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open-raise. I will raise to 3x the big blind to try and steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot in position with a highly playable hand.

My hand, 2d 5h, is extremely weak and offers very little playability. Being in the Small Blind position means I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the button-raiser. Calling or raising here with such a disconnected low-card hand is strategically unsound. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of roughly 3.25 to 1. In a three-handed game, Ace-high hands have significant equity against a standard Button opening range. Folding would be too conservative, and while 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing out of position with a weak kicker.
I've checked to the pre-flop aggressor on a dry Queen-high board. My Ace-high has limited value here, and I'm out of position. Checking allows me to see how the Button reacts.

On a dry Q-high flop, my range as the pre-flop raiser from the Button hits well, containing many strong Queens and overpairs. Meanwhile, the Big Blind's range is mostly composed of weaker hands that missed the board. With 87 of clubs, I have almost no showdown value but hold backdoor flush and straight draws. This makes it an ideal hand to continuation bet as a semi-bluff. Betting around 40% of the pot should fold out BB's air and weaker holdings while keeping my bluff cheap.

On this dry Qd 3h 6c board, I have Ace-high. Pro (the Button) has made a continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot. While my hand is currently just Ace-high, on such a disconnected board, a standard c-bet from the pre-flop aggressor is extremely common and doesn't necessarily indicate a Queen or a pocket pair. I am getting 4.6 to 1 on a call (250 to win 1150 total). Given these odds and the fact that I might have the best hand or can potentially bluff on later streets if the action checks through on the turn, I will call one bet to see a turn card.

The 8s on the turn is a blank card that doesn't improve my Ace-high. After calling a continuation bet on the flop, I will check to the aggressor to see how he proceeds. If he bets again, I will likely fold, but checking allows the possibility of seeing a free river or catching a check-back.

The turn 8s gives me middle pair. My opponent called a continuation bet on a Q-high flop, indicating a range that likely includes Qx, mid-pocket pairs (like 99-JJ), 6x, or floats. Betting here serves little purpose as a value bet or a bluff: worse hands (like 44-55 or 6x) will likely fold, while better hands (like Qx or higher pocket pairs) will call. Checking back controls the pot size, takes a free card, and allows me to realize my showdown value or improve on the river.

On a board of Qd 3h 6c 8s Qs, I have Ace-high. Pre-flop, the Button (Pro) raised and I called. On the flop, Pro bet small and I called. The turn went check-check. Pro's check on the turn suggests a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to build a big pot, or a hand that has completely given up. With the river pairing the Queen, it's less likely Pro has a Queen. My Ace-high has some showdown value against his air and missed draws. I will check to see if he checks back and allows me to see a showdown for free. If he bets, I will likely fold as his turn check-back often indicates a small pair or a marginal hand that beats my Ace-high.

On the river, the board pairs the Queen, and my opponent checks again. I hold two pair (Queens and Eights). Since I checked back the turn, my opponent's river check usually indicates marginal showdown value like a 6x, lower pocket pairs (44-55), or a busted straight draw, though they could occasionally be trapping with a Queen or holding a mid-pocket pair like 99-JJ. A value bet is very thin here because worse hands will likely fold, and better hands will call. Checking back allows me to safely realize my showdown value and win at showdown against missed draws or weaker pairs.

I am holding 4c-2s, which is an extremely weak hand with no playability. Even in position on the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into this pot. I will fold to conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 6h. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). However, since the pot is small and I want to see how Pro reacts, I will raise to 300 to exert pressure.

I am dealt an extremely weak hand, 8h 5s, offsuit and disconnected. Facing a 3x raise from the small blind, even with positional advantage post-flop, calling or raising with this hand is unprofitable over the long run. Folding is the only optimal decision.
KTo is a strong opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 3x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early, while playing in position.

Flash is on the button and likely opening a wide range of hands. A6s is a great candidate for a 3-bet from the small blind; it blocks strong Aces, has suited potential to make the nut flush, and folds out many of Flash's marginal steals. Sizing up to 1000 to compensate for being out of position.

I am holding Ten-Three offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. Facing an initial raise and a 3-bet from the small blind, my hand is clearly dominated. Investing 900 chips, which represents a significant portion of my 5300-chip stack, to play out of position against two aggressive opponents is strategically unsound. The best course of action is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Raising again (4-betting) would likely commit me to a stack-off situation where I am rarely a favorite. Therefore, calling the 700 to see a flop in position is the most balanced play.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a significant range advantage on this dry, paired board, having all the overpairs (AA-TT) which Flash would often 4-bet pre-flop. With Ac6c, we also have some backdoor flush and straight equity, plus an overcard, making this a good candidate to bet and maintain the initiative.

On this dry, paired board of 5-5-9, Pro's 1/3 pot continuation bet is standard for a 3-bet pot. While I only have King-high, I have two overcards that are likely live against many of his 3-betting bluffs or semi-bluffs (like AQ, AJ, or smaller suited connectors). Folding to such a small bet in position would be too exploitable. On the turn, the board is 5c 5h 9d 7c. Pro has followed up his pre-flop 3-bet and flop continuation bet with a second, larger barrel of 1800 into a pot of 3500. Calling a second barrel with King-high in a 3-bet pot is extremely risky and lacks sufficient equity or a clear path to winning the hand unless I bluff later, which is difficult given the stack sizes. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.
A9o on the Button is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. I will open to 3x the big blind (300) to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop against callers.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Three suited, which is a playable hand. The button (Pro) raised to 300. With decent pot odds, I will call the 250 to see a flop. Playing out of position dictates caution, but A3s has good post-flop potential and is too strong to fold here.

I am out of position against the button, and the flop of Ten-Seven-Five is completely uncoordinated with my Ace-Three suited. Without a pair or a strong draw, I have very little equity here. Checking is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's action and control the pot size.

I have Ace-high with the Ace of spades, giving me a backdoor flush draw. The turn card (Six of Spades) completes a potential flush draw and makes the board very wet with straight possibilities. The turn brings a third spade and a connected board, completing many draws. However, Flashlight's second check indicates weakness, likely meaning he doesn't have a flush or a strong straight. Even if I get called, I have plenty of equity with 9 outs to the nut flush and potentially 3 more outs for top pair. Betting around 60% of the pot puts the right amount of pressure on his marginal holdings.

I am facing a bet of 400 into a pot of 1100 on a turn board that has three spades. I hold Ace of Hearts and Three of Hearts, giving me absolutely no flush equity, no straight draw, and no pair. Calling would be a significant mistake as I am out of position and have no way to improve to a winning hand unless I spike an Ace, which is likely still behind. Folding is the only disciplined play.

Qh 8d is a weak, marginal hand that does not play well against the 3-handed ranges of my opponents. Given my short stack of 5000 chips, I need to be selective with my starting hands to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities, rather than risking chips with a sub-optimal hand.

In the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit after the Button has folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. J9o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to maintain initiative.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. My hand, 2d 5d, is suited but severely disconnected and low, making it practically unplayable even in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Calling or 3-betting here is not profitable. Folding is the correct move.

I am on the button with Ten of Spades and Four of Hearts (T4o). This is a very weak hand, well outside the standard opening range even in a 3-handed game. With a short stack in the Big Blind (Flashlight with 49 BBs) who may defend aggressively or shove, and a capable player in the Small Blind, there is no reason to attempt a steal with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
With Q6o in the small blind, the hand is too weak to justify a limp or a raise out of position against the big blind. The best move is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.
I'm on the Button with 7h 4d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (2d, 6c). With no action from the button and the big blind still to act, there is no strategic value in investing more chips into this pot out of position with such poor cards. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my remaining stack of 5000.

I am on the button with 8c 5c, which is a playable speculative hand in a short-handed game. My stack is significantly shorter than my opponents', so I need to be active to avoid being blinded down. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening move to attempt a steal of the blinds, putting pressure on the small and big blinds to fold, while maintaining a manageable pot size if they call.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a standard raise. Since Flashlight has a relatively short stack (47.5 BBs) and I am out of position, I want to 3-bet to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or isolate the short stack. Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, K9o is too weak of a hand to play. It has significant reverse implied odds and we would be playing out of position against the Button if they call. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I opened to 250 and was 3-bet to 850 by the Small Blind. With 8c 5c, I have a suited connector that has decent playability and implied odds, even against a 3-bet. While it's not a premium holding, the pot odds (getting 2:1 on the call) and my position justify continuing to see a flop. I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Jack and have the range advantage on this dry Queen-high flop. While I missed the board, my range contains many strong hands (AA, KK, QQ, AQ) that Flashlight's calling range struggles against. A 1/3 pot continuation bet is standard here to maintain aggression and potentially win the pot immediately against hands like middle pocket pairs or smaller Ace-highs.

The board texture is 3s-Qd-2c, which completely missed my hand of 8c-5c. While I have backdoor flush potential, the likelihood of improving to the best hand against an aggressive opponent is low. Ten-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Despite being on the button, the hand lacks any significant equity or post-flop playability. With Flashlight on a relatively short stack in the Big Blind, I expect them to defend or shove more aggressively, making a steal attempt with trash cards unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

My hand has decent showdown value in a limped pot, but it's not strong enough to bet for value reliably or to turn into a bluff. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free, control the size of the pot, and potentially catch a bluff or a worse hand later.

I am in the small blind with Queen-high on a King-high board. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. My hand has effectively zero equity against a calling range, and there is no reason to bluff here as the big blind likely checked the flop with a range that includes many pairs or better. Checking is the only sensible move to control the pot size and minimize potential losses.

In a limped pot against the Small Blind, I have middle pair (fives) with a Jack kicker. Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting they don't have a strong King or potentially even an eight. While I could bet for value or to protect against overcards, my hand has solid showdown value in a small pot. Checking back the turn allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or catch a bluff on the river, especially given Flashlight's short stack.

I have Queen-high on a board of K-5-8-6-9. This hand has no showdown value. Since the pot has been limped and checked down through every street, there is no reason to turn this into a bluff. Checking is the only logical action to conclude the hand.

I have a pair of fives on a board with a King and several middle cards. In this limped pot, the Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked every street, indicating a weak hand or a modest showdown hand. While my pair of fives is now the third or fourth pair on this board, it still retains significant showdown value in a pot where no one has shown any aggression. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands and could potentially face a check-raise from a hand that improved on the later streets. Checking back ensures I reach showdown for free in a small pot.

My hand, Queen-Deuce offsuit, is extremely weak and provides very little equity in a multi-way pot. Given my short stack size relative to the blinds, I need to exercise discipline and wait for a higher-quality hand to make a move or steal the blinds. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. While a steal attempt against the Big Blind (Pro) is sometimes viable, T4o is a very weak hand to play out of position if called. Pro has shown a willingness to play back in previous hands. I am on the button with pocket fives, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. Flashlight is quite short-stacked with about 24.5 big blinds, while Pro has a stack similar to mine. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300) is a consistent play that applies pressure to the blinds. If Flashlight shoves, I'll have to consider the pot odds, but for now, I want to take control of the hand.

We hold King-Queen suited in the Small Blind facing a Button open. This is a premium hand 3-handed. A 3-bet is preferred over a call to build the pot for value, gain the betting lead, and avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position if the Big Blind decides to call or squeeze. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing out of position that puts pressure on both the Button and the short-stacked Big Blind.

Facing a raise and a re-raise (3-bet) from the Small Blind with an offsuit Jack-Eight is an easy fold. These cards are significantly dominated by the likely ranges of the other two players, and calling would commit a large portion of my dwindling stack without a clear path to winning the pot. Conservatism is necessary here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I opened with a pocket pair of fives on the button, and Pro has responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. Given that we are both deep-stacked (over 120 big blinds effective), set mining is a mathematically sound strategy here. I am getting roughly 2:1 immediate pot odds, but the high implied odds of hitting a set against a 3-betting range make a call profitable, especially since I will have position for the remainder of the hand. I'll call the 700 difference and evaluate the flop.

As the pre-flop 3-bettor, the Ace-high flop heavily favors our range. C-betting small (around 1/3 pot) is a standard play to apply pressure, fold out better high cards or underpairs, and maintain the betting lead. Even with just King-Queen high, we have strong blockers and can barrel favorable turns.

I called the pre-flop 3-bet with pocket fives primarily looking to hit a set. The flop of Ah-9h-3d is highly favorable for Pro's 3-betting range, which is dense with high pairs and strong Aces. Continuing here would be unprofitable in the long run against a professional opponent who is representing a range that crushes mine. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the Button with K5s (Kd 5d). This is a reasonable hand to open-raise from the button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to put pressure on both opponents, particularly the short-stacked small blind.

I have been dealt Pocket Kings, a top-tier premium hand. As the short stack (2400 chips) at the table, I need to play aggressively to maximize the value of this hand. Calling would allow the Big Blind to potentially see a cheap flop, creating a multi-way pot where Kings are harder to navigate out of position. By shoving all-in, I put maximum pressure on the opener, fold out marginal hands, and get the best chance to double up or take down the pot immediately.

Flashlight has made a massive 3-bet to 2400 chips, which represents nearly their entire stack (only 50 chips remaining). This is essentially a shove. Given the significant price to call (2300 more) and my disadvantageous position and hand strength against a polarized or strong shoving range, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my healthy stack of 13,050.

I opened K5s on the button as a standard steal attempt, but facing a very large 3-bet to 2400 from the small blind, my hand is far too weak to continue. Folding is the only sensible option here to preserve my stack.

My starting hand, 7s 2d, is mathematically one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. With a very short stack of 2850 relative to the blinds, I must be extremely selective about the hands I play to maximize my survival chances in the tournament. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such a poor hand. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited (Td 8d). This is a strong hand for a blind-versus-blind scenario. I am in the Big Blind with T3o, a very weak unsuited hand. The Small Blind has raised 3x to 300. Even though I would have position post-flop, this hand is simply too weak to defend against a raise. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

In Hand #59, I am on the button with Jack of Hearts and Trey of Spades. This is a very weak hand (J3offsuit), which does not play well post-flop and lacks high-card value. I am in the small blind with Q6s and facing the big blind who has a 27.5 BB stack. Q6s is a marginal hand and the big blind has shown capability for aggressive 3-bets (as seen in Hand #57). To avoid getting pushed off my equity or playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak hand, folding is the safest and most optimal choice here.

Jc9d is an unsuited, disconnected hand (one-gapper) that plays poorly against 3-bets and post-flop when called. From the button, while a steal is possible, Flashlight has a relatively short stack and might be inclined to shove or defend aggressively, and Flash in the Big Blind is deep enough to defend or 3-bet. The button has folded, leaving me in a blind-vs-blind situation against the big blind. Holding Queen-Eight suited, I have a playable hand that can extract value or take down the blinds pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take initiative and put pressure on the big blind, given my stack is relatively short.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand (93o). Flashlight, who is the short stack at the table with only 26BB, has raised from the small blind. I am on the button with King-Eight suited in a three-handed game. This is a strong position, and K8s is a viable hand to open with to attempt to steal the blinds and play in position. I will raise to 300 to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3c. I have pocket sevens in the Big Blind facing a Button open from Flashlight. Flashlight has around 30 BBs remaining. I have the second-nut flush draw (Kd) on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, betting is the standard move to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot immediately. Even if called, I have strong equity to improve my hand.

I am on the button with KQo, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, so I should be prepared for a potential shove, but KQo is well ahead of their likely defending range.
Holding Ten-Four offsuit in the small blind against a button raise is an easy decision. The hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop, making it a clear fold.

I am out of position on the flop with 6h9h on a 3h-Js-4c board. My hand has very little equity, holding only a backdoor flush draw and no pair or significant straight draw. I am in the Big Blind with 53o. The Button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) just completed the blind. I have the option to check and see a free flop with a very weak hand. There is no strategic advantage to raising here given the hand strength and the fact that I can see the flop for free.

I am sitting in the small blind with 9-7 offsuit on a board of Jack-high. I have essentially no equity or pair value. Since this was a limped pot, there is no reason to lead out with a bluff. Checking is the standard play to see if the big blind wants to take a free card or betting lead.

I am in the Big Blind with 53o. The pot is very small (200), and Flashlight has checked to me. My hand has completely missed this high-card flop (Jh-2c-Qs). I have 53o and have completely missed the board of Jh-2c-Qs-6d. The small blind checked to me, and there is no reason to bluff into a small pot with zero equity when I can see the river for free. I will check.

I hold 9-high on an Ace-high board with no pair or draw potential. I have 53o and have completely missed the board of Jh-2c-Qs-6d-Ad. The small blind checked to me on the river in a pot that has been checked down the whole way. 45o is a very weak hand, even when defending the Big Blind against a Small Blind open. Playing this hand post-flop will usually put us in tough spots without much equity. Folding is the standard and correct play.

The flop of Jack-Eight-King has completely missed my Ten-Five offsuit. I have no pair and no meaningful draws. As the first player to act on the flop, checking is the only strategic option. I will look to fold if the button player decides to continuation bet.

The board is Jack-Eight-King and I hold Ten-Five suited, meaning I have no pair and no draw. My opponent has made a continuation bet into me. Holding 92 offsuit on the button, this is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There is no strategic justification to play this hand, so folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am in the small blind with a small pocket pair (33) against the big blind. Given my short stack, I want to see a cheap flop without bloating the pot unnecessarily with a vulnerable hand. Calling the remaining 50 chips is the optimal play to see if I can improve.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight completed the small blind, which typically indicates a wide and often weak range. I am holding a pocket pair of 3s in the small blind. The Big Blind has raised to 400 after I completed the small blind. I am in the small blind holding a pocket pair of 3s on a board of Queen-Jack-Two. My pair is extremely weak here, and I have completely missed the flop. I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board of Q-J-2. I am in the small blind with a pair of 3s on a board of Queen-Jack-Two. My pair is extremely weak, and the opponent, who showed strength pre-flop by raising, has continuation-bet into me. Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand. Even though it's a blind versus blind situation and I would have position post-flop, the hand lacks playability and equity to justify calling a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am on the button with King-Trey suited (Kd 3d), which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (74o). Flash has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a very clear and straightforward fold pre-flop.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a relatively dry, disconnected board. I raised pre-flop with King-Trey suited and was called by Flashlight in the Big Blind. The flop (T-5-2 rainbow) is very dry and favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. I have top pair with a Ten, which is a strong hand on this dry, disconnected board of [5h, 2s, Tc]. The board is 5h 2s Tc 7h. I hold Td 9s, giving me a pair of Tens. This is a relatively dry board, and as the big blind, I prefer to keep the pot controlled rather than leading out. I have King-high on a board of 10-5-2-7. Flashlight has check-called the flop, which suggests they have a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a Five, or a pocket pair. I am in the big blind holding a pair of Tens (Td 9s) on a board of 5h 2s Tc 7h Qh. The turn was checked through, indicating that Flash likely does not have a monster hand or is pot-controlling. Checking is the best move to control the pot, potentially see a showdown if Flash checks behind, or fold if Flash makes a substantial bet.

Flashlight check-called the flop, which suggests they have some part of the board, likely a Ten, a Seven, or a small pocket pair. K7o is a marginal offsuit hand. While it is technically within a standard 3-handed button opening range, folding is a very safe and solid play here. It avoids facing a potential 3-bet from the big stack in the Big Blind and prevents us from getting into tricky post-flop spots with a weak kicker.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h-2d, which is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. Raising from the button is a standard move to attempt to steal the blinds and take the initiative in the hand, especially in a three-handed game.

My hand is 6s 2h, which is very weak, uncoordinated, and offsuit. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button, playing this trash hand out of position is highly unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible play.

With Flash folding on the button, I am in the Small Blind with K9o, which is a reasonably strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flashlight's 30 BB stack and often wins the blinds outright. If called, I have a decent high-card hand to play post-flop.

AK offsuit is a premium starting hand. Being first to act on the Button, I want to open with a standard 3x raise to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially isolate the blinds or win the pot outright.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven of Diamonds, a very strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind situation after the Button has folded.
