A6s is a solid suited Ace that functions well as a 3-bet bluff/value hybrid from the small blind against a button open. By 3-betting to 1000, I apply maximum pressure to the button's wide opening range, seeking to take down the pot uncontested pre-flop or gain the betting initiative. It also heavily discourages the short-stacked big blind from attempting a squeeze.

My hand [2h, 3d] is extremely weak and has very little showdown value. There has been a raise to 300 and a 3-bet to 1000 in front of me. Investing 900 chips—nearly one-third of my remaining 2900 stack—with this hand is a clear negative EV play. I need to conserve my stack and wait for a significantly better opportunity to double up.

I opened J8o from the button and faced a 3-bet to 1000 from Pro in the Small Blind. While I have position, J8o is too weak to continue against a 3-bet from a competent opponent. My hand is dominated by a typical 3-betting range, and it doesn't have enough playability or raw equity to justify calling 700 more. I will fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

I am on the Button (first to act pre-flop in a 3-handed game) with 4h 5s. This is a very weak, unsuited, low-ranking hand. It is not profitable to open-raise or limp with this hand, so the optimal choice is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind position, and the Button (Pro) has folded. The Big Blind is 100, and I have already posted 50. With T9s, completing the blind (calling the additional 50) is a standard and profitable play in a heads-up scenario to see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five of diamonds. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind. Flashlight is playing a relatively short stack of 2800 chips (28 BBs). While T5s has some playability, it is not strong enough to warrant a raise for value or as a pure bluff against a short stack who might be looking to jam over a raise. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and realize my equity without increasing the pot size out of position.

I am in the small blind with Ten-Nine offsuit on an Ace-high board with two clubs. I have no made hand and no significant draw, just a gutshot (actually, no, I have 10-high, no draw). Checking is the standard play here to control the pot size since I am out of position and have no real equity to justify a bet.

The flop is monotone with three clubs (8c Ac 3c), and I hold the Ten-Five of diamonds, which has no interaction with this board. Flashlight checked to me. In a limped pot, there is little incentive to bluff on such a coordinated board where any club or any Ace in the opponent's range has significant equity or a made hand. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and keep the pot small, which is the most prudent play with zero current hand strength and no draw.

I am in the Small Blind position with Ten-Nine offsuit on a board showing 8c-Ac-3c-Qs. The turn has just been dealt, and I am first to act. I have no made hand and no significant draw, meaning my showdown value is nonexistent. Checking is the most strategic play here to control the pot size and see a free river card, as there is no reason to put more chips into the pot with a weak hand.

I am in a limped pot with Ten-high on a very coordinated board (8c Ac 3c Qs). Flashlight has checked twice, which indicates weakness, but my own hand has zero showdown value and almost no equity to improve. While a bluff might work, the pot is very small (200 chips) and Flashlight's short stack makes them less likely to fold if they have even a small piece of the board or a single club. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and avoid unnecessary risk in a marginal situation.

The board is 8c-Ac-3c-Qs-8s, and I hold Th-9s. My hand has absolutely no showdown value, as I do not even have a pair. Any bet I make would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed, especially with the Ace and Queen on the board. Checking is the optimal strategy to minimize potential losses and see the opponent's hand if they choose to show.

The board is 8c Ac 3c Qs 8s and the pot is very small (200 chips) after both players checked the flop and turn. I have Ten-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, Flashlight has checked for the third time, indicating a very weak range. While a small bluff could potentially take the pot, in a limped pot with such low stakes, it is often better to just check back and take the free showdown rather than risking chips to win a negligible amount, especially since even a small pair or a better high card from the opponent would call a small bet.

Queen-Deuce suited is a weak hand in a three-handed game, even when holding the button position. With a short stack of 2900 chips, playing marginal hands out of position or in speculative situations is not optimal. Preserving my remaining stack and waiting for a more high-equity hand is the disciplined strategic choice.

I am in the small blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Since the button has folded, it is just me and the big blind. However, 8-2 offsuit has very little equity and playing it out of position against a strong opponent like 'Pro' is not a winning strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit. While this is a potential opening hand in a 3-handed game, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked with only 28 big blinds. If I raise and he shoves, I would be forced to fold a significant portion of my opening range. 9-7 offsuit is too weak to call a shove and not strong enough to justify an open-fold strategy against this specific stack depth. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Action folded to me in the small blind. I hold King-Eight offsuit, which is well within my opening range for a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising to 300 (3 big blinds) is a standard steal attempt that puts pressure on the big blind, who has a somewhat short stack, forcing them to either defend with a marginal hand or surrender the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind raised to 300, and the Button folded. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 to win a 400 pot). J9o is a reasonable hand to defend with in the Big Blind against a single raise, especially since I will have position post-flop. Folding would be too tight given the odds and my position. I will call to see the flop.

The flop is very dry and rainbow (2d 5s 9c), which favors my pre-flop raising range. Although I completely missed the flop and only have King-high, a small continuation bet will frequently fold out my opponent's unpaired overcards and missed connectors. Given the opponent's relatively short stack, a bet of around 40% pot puts enough pressure on their marginal hands without overcommitting my own stack.

I am in the Big Blind with top pair (Nines) on a dry board (2d, 5s, 9c). The Small Blind (Pro) has continued with a small bet of 250 into a 600-chip pot. My hand is strong enough to call here as I am likely ahead of their continuation bet range, and the dry board texture doesn't present many immediate threats. I have position on the Pro, allowing me to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the turn based on their next move.

The turn pairs the deuce and brings a backdoor flush draw. Since my opponent called my continuation bet on a very dry flop, they likely have at least a pair or a strong float. Given their short stack, a double barrel here is risky as they are unlikely to fold any made hand. I only have King high, so checking controls the pot size and allows me to re-evaluate based on their action, saving chips for better spots.

The board is dry (2d 5s 9c 2s) and the opponent (Pro) has checked the turn, signaling weakness or a hand that has given up. My top pair with a Jack kicker is very likely the best hand here. I need to bet for value to get paid by weaker pairs like 66, 77, 88 or potential draws, while also protecting against overcards. A bet of 500 into the 1100 pot is a solid size to build value without over-committing if the opponent check-raises.

My opponent called my continuation bet on the flop and has now bet on the turn after I checked. Given their short stack, this bet demonstrates significant strength or a committed draw. I only hold King-high with no viable draws to improve to a strong hand. Calling would be a losing play, and raising as a bluff is too risky against a committed short stack. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision.

I am on the Button with 7s 3s, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Given the stack sizes, particularly Flashlight's short stack in the small blind which increases the likelihood of a jam, folding is the standard and safest play.

The button folded, and I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. With K9o, I have a reasonable hand to contest the pot, but my shorter stack warrants a cautious approach. I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 5c. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has completed the blind. With a mediocre hand like Q5 offsuit, there is no reason to raise and bloat the pot, especially against a shorter stack. I will take the free flop and see if I can improve my hand.

I am in the small blind with King-high and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a Queen) on a coordinated J-T-3 board. Since the big blind checked, the pot is small (200), and I have limited showdown value, checking is the optimal strategy to realize my equity for free and avoid inflating the pot with a weak holding.
I have Queen-high on a Jack-Ten-Trey board. The Small Blind checked to me. There's no reason to bet here with no pair and no real draw. I'll check and take the free card.

The turn card (Queen of Spades) completes my straight (9-T-J-Q-K). While I am vulnerable to an Ace-King for a higher straight, I have a very strong hand on this board. I will bet 150 into the 200 pot to extract value from any Jacks, Tens, or straight draws that might call.

I have turned top pair (Queens) on a board of 3h-Th-Jd-Qs. While the board is quite coordinated and many straight draws are possible (like 9-8 or K-9), Flashlight's small bet of 150 into a 200 pot (pre-turn) suggests they might be testing the waters with a Jack, a Ten, or a draw. I'm getting good odds to call (150 to win 500) and it's too early to fold top pair, but raising might overplay my hand given the straight possibilities. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

The board is 3h-Th-Jd-Qs-Kd. My hand is 9d-Ks, giving me a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). Any opponent holding an Ace would have a higher straight (Ace-high), while anyone holding a 9 would also have the same King-high straight, resulting in a chop. Given that there are no obvious worse hands that would call a bet, and I risk being beaten by an Ace or chopping with a 9, checking is the most strategic move to reach showdown without risking additional chips.

The board has become extremely coordinated with Ten-Jack-Queen-King now showing. Any Ace or any Nine completes a straight. My pair of Queens is now a very marginal hand, especially since any King also beats me. Since Flashlight checked, there is no value in betting; I likely won't be called by worse hands, and I certainly don't want to be check-raised by a straight or a King. I will check behind and hope my pair is still the best hand at showdown.

With Ace-Four offsuit on the button, I have a reasonable hand to open the pot. With 37 big blinds, it is important to be proactive and attempt to steal the blinds or take the initiative pre-flop. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

KQo is a premium hand in 3-handed play. I am facing a button open from Flashlight, who has a relatively short stack (34BB). A 3-bet to 900 allows me to take control of the hand, potentially fold out the Big Blind, and put pressure on the opener. Calling is an option, but playing KQo out of position is better done with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak suited hand (2h5h). Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, calling or 4-betting here would be a huge overplay. The most profitable and standard play is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind, my Ace-Four offsuit is still a decent holding in position. Given the pot odds and my stack depth, calling the additional 600 chips to see a flop is the most balanced approach. I do not have a strong enough hand to 4-bet shove, but I am too deep to fold an Ace-high hand immediately. I will call to evaluate the flop and maintain my position.

On a board of Jack-Ten-Four, I have a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine) and two overcards (King and Queen). This board texture heavily favors my pre-flop 3-betting range. Since the pot is 1,900 and Flashlight has 2,800 remaining, a bet of 950 (half pot) puts maximum pressure on his medium-strength hands like pocket pairs (22-99) or weak Ten-X hands. With 14 outs to improve, I have roughly 48% equity to hit a better hand by the river, making a bet here highly profitable as both a bluff and a way to build the pot for my draws.

With bottom pair (fours) on a J-T-4 board, my hand is extremely vulnerable to many of the hands in Flash's 3-bet range (such as overpairs, or broadway combos like KQ, QJ, or JT). A 950 bet into a 1900 pot is a significant commitment. Calling would leave me with only 950 chips behind, effectively pot-committing me to a weak hand against a range that likely has me crushed. Preserving my remaining stack of 1900 is the most prudent tournament strategy here rather than chasing a marginal pair with limited equity.

I am on the button with KQo, which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a large stack and want to exert pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is quite short-stacked with only 27 BBs, while Pro has a stack similar to mine. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak, disconnected offsuit hand (3h 9d). Facing a raise from the Button, the most mathematically sound and standard play is to fold and save my chips.

My hand, 5d 3s, is very weak and lacks sufficient equity to defend against an aggressive button raise, even with the pot odds provided. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here to preserve my remaining stack.
I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a standard hand to open-raise from late position. I will make it 3x the big blind to steal the blinds or play a pot in position if called.

With A7o in the small blind, I have a decent hand to defend against the button's raise, especially given the pot odds. With a short stack of 2650, I need to look for opportunities to play pots and double up. Calling 250 to see a flop is a solid, standard play that keeps the pot manageable while maintaining potential to win the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 7s. Pro raised to 300 from the Button and Flashlight called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to see a flop. While 67o is a weak hand, the price is too good to pass up in a multiway pot, especially since I am closing the pre-flop action. I will call the additional 200 chips to see if I can connect with the flop.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw on a relatively dry board. With two opponents and being out of position, checking is the most strategic play to re-evaluate on later streets. Betting here would likely be a bluff that accomplishes little against two opponents.

I have 6h 7s on a 2h Td 5d flop. I have no pair and very little draw potential. Flashlight has checked to me. Pro was the pre-flop aggressor and is still to act behind me. There is no reason to bet here with air in a multi-way pot. I will check and see how the action develops, likely folding to any significant bet from Pro.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board with flush and straight draws. Since both opponents checked to me, it is a clear spot to bet for value and protection. A bet of half pot to two-thirds pot will extract value from worse tens, underpairs, and various draws while making them pay to see the next card.

I hold Ace-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw on a relatively dry board of 2h, Td, 5d. With the opponent (Pro) on the button betting 450 into a 900 pot, I am getting 2:1 odds on a call. My Ace is often the best hand on this board texture, and although I am out of position, folding would be too conservative. I will call to see the turn, where I can re-evaluate if the board remains favourable or if I pick up more equity.

On this 2h Td 5d flop, I have 7-high with no significant draws. Pro has made a continuation bet of 450, and Flashlight has called. Given that Flashlight is short-stacked (leaving only 1500 behind) and has already committed chips to the pot, they are likely to have a piece of the board or a draw. I have almost zero equity in this multi-way pot and no reason to continue. Preserving my stack is the priority here.

The turn card (5c) pairs the board, which might have improved a hand like a pair of 5s or maybe nothing at all. With Ace-high out of position, checking is the most sensible play to see if the opponent checks behind or bets. Betting is too risky as a pure bluff, and folding is premature since my hand still has potential showdown value if the opponent is also bluffing. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the board pairs the 5, which doesn't change much unless Flashlight specifically holds a 5. I still have top pair with a good Jack kicker. Flashlight checked again and only has 1,950 chips behind with the pot at 1,800. To extract value from diamond flush draws, worse tens, and underpairs, and to deny free equity, I need to bet. A half-pot bet of 900 charges his draws heavily and sets up a manageable river decision or an all-in.

The opponent's consistent aggression on both the flop and the turn indicates a strong range that likely has me beaten. The board has paired with the 5c, further solidifying the board texture and reducing the likelihood that my Ace-high will catch up or is currently the best hand. With no made hand and no significant draw, calling a 900 chip bet into a 2700 pot is mathematically unsound and risks a large portion of my remaining stack. It is the most disciplined and professional decision to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with QTo, a hand that is definitely strong enough to open in a three-handed game. Given my relatively short stack of 1950 chips compared to the much larger stacks of my opponents, I need to play aggressively to increase my stack. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard move to take control of the hand, potentially stealing the blinds or setting up a favorable position post-flop.

I have Queen-Jack offsuit in the small blind, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight, the button, has raised to 300 but is playing with a very short stack (only 1,650 total). I have a comfortable stack and want to see a flop with this hand. Calling is the best option here to see how the board develops and how the big blind (Pro) reacts. Folding is too weak given the hand strength and the pot odds, and raising might force out hands I dominate or commit me against a short-stack shove that I'd rather evaluate after seeing a flop.

With T7 suited in the Big Blind, facing a button raise and a small blind call, our hand is too weak to defend. Furthermore, the button (Flashlight) is very short-stacked (around 16.5 BBs behind), meaning the SPR on the flop will be very low, limiting our implied odds and preventing us from realizing equity with drawing hands. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

On this dry flop of 3d-Qc-8s, I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows Flashlight to continuation bet with a wide range of hands, including air or weaker pairs. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack, I want to keep the pot accessible for him to commit chips. If he bets, I will certainly continue. Checking is more likely to extract value from bluffs or weaker hands than leading out here.

I have top pair with Queen-Ten on a dry board of [3d, Qc, 8s]. My opponent has checked, indicating they likely don't have a strong hand or are checking to trap. With a short stack relative to my opponents and the blinds, I need to be aggressive. Betting half the pot (350 chips) allows me to gain value from any weaker Queens or middle pairs while also protecting my hand against potential draws. This also maintains a solid stack-to-pot ratio for future streets if they call.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a very dry flop (Q-8-3). This is a very strong hand, especially against a short-stacked opponent like Flashlight who only has 1,300 chips left. I chose to check the flop to let him continue with his pre-flop aggression. Now that he has bet 350, calling is the most profitable play. It keeps his entire range in, including bluffs and weaker pairs that he might fold if I were to raise. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR), I am prepared to commit the rest of my chips on the turn, but calling now maximizes the chance he will bluff off his remaining stack on later streets.

I have flopped top pair and turned trips with a Jack kicker. My opponent, Flashlight, has a very short stack of 1,300 chips remaining with a pot already at 1,400. By checking, I continue the narrative of having a marginal hand (like an 8 or a pocket pair) which I established by check-calling the flop. This maximizes the probability that Flashlight will commit his remaining chips as a bluff or a thin value bet with a weaker hand. Leading out might cause him to fold his total air, whereas checking keeps his entire range in play. Given the SPR is approximately 1, I am essentially playing for his entire stack regardless of the action, but checking is the best way to induce an error.

I have flopped trips with my Queen-Ten on a board of 3d-Qc-8s-Qs, which is an extremely strong hand. The pot currently sits at 1300 chips and the opponent has checked to me on the turn. I want to build the pot and extract value from any weaker pairs or draws they might be holding. Given the board texture and my strong hand, a bet of 800 is a solid value bet that puts pressure on the opponent and maximizes my equity.

I have turned three of a kind with a Jack kicker on a Queen-high board. Flashlight is extremely short-stacked, with only 500 chips remaining behind his 800-chip turn bet. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that Flashlight is effectively pot-committed, raising to 1300 (putting him all-in) is the most logical play to ensure I maximize value and secure the rest of his stack immediately. There is no benefit to slow-playing here since the pot-to-stack ratio is so low.

I have trips (Queens) with my Queen-Ten on the board of 3d-Qc-8s-Qs. While there is a risk that my opponent could have a full house (such as 33 or 88), the pot is 3500 and I only need to call 500 to stay in. With only 500 chips remaining in my stack, I am pot-committed, and the potential value of my hand far outweighs the risk of folding. A call is the only logical and mathematically sound play.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with 5s 4s, which is a strong speculative hand for a wide opening range. Raising is standard here to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind.

Facing a button open from Flash, pocket fours are a standard flat call in the big blind. With almost 140 big blinds effective, we have plenty of stack depth to set-mine or occasionally take down the pot post-flop. 3-betting a small pair out of position can lead to tricky situations without hitting a set.

On a King-high board with a flush draw, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play. Our pocket fours have some marginal showdown value, but betting would mostly just fold out hands we currently beat while getting called or raised by better hands and strong draws.

On the flop of 3h 7s Ks, I have a flush draw (5s 4s with 7s Ks on board) and a gutshot straight draw (3-4-5 needing a 6). Pro checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. This is a perfect spot for a continuation bet. I represent the King well, and even if called, I have significant equity to improve on the turn or river. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard to maintain pressure and build the pot for my draws.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet on a King-high board, my underpair (pocket fours) has very little equity against the pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position with only two true outs to improve to a set, continuing is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
92o is a very weak hand, being unsuited and disconnected. From the small blind, this is a standard and easy fold pre-flop.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Ten-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the big blind. My stack is healthy at 16,250 chips (162.5 BBs), and my opponent has 13,600 chips. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand early and potentially win the blinds immediately. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size.

I hold 62o, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the button, there is absolutely no reason to defend this holding. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack.

Q7o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. In a heads-up scenario from the small blind/button, while we can play a wide range, folding these lower-kicker offsuit queens is a solid default to avoid marginal situations post-flop where we could be dominated.

I am on the button (SB) with Qc 4c. This is a solid suited-queen hand, which is well above the average range for heads-up or short-handed play. Against 'Pro' in the Big Blind, I want to apply pressure and maintain an aggressive image. A standard 3x open (total 300) is appropriate here to take control of the pot early or fold out weaker hands from the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with 96o, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Flash has raised to 3 big blinds from the Button. Playing 96o out of position against a raise is a significantly losing proposition, so the clear decision is to fold.

75 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even in heads-up play from the button. It lacks high card strength, suitedness, and connectivity. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with J6o. My opponent, Pro, has been folding their big blind frequently in recent hands. While J6o is a relatively weak starting hand, standard heads-up strategy dictates opening a very wide range from the button, especially against a tight opponent. I will raise to 300 to maintain aggression and attempt to take down the blinds.

Q8o is too weak of an offsuit hand to defend against a 3x open raise from the button, especially since we'll be out of position post-flop. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 9d 3s, which is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. It is not worth investing any chips, even with positional advantage post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

In heads-up play, any King-high hand is well above the median starting hand strength. Given that the opponent (Pro) has folded to my button raises in several recent hands (Hand #85, #87, #89), a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate to take down the blinds or define the hand range early. King-three offsuit plays well enough against a single opponent if called.

K8o is a weak offsuit hand, especially when out of position against a 3x open raise from the button. Although our opponent opens frequently, defending K8o will lead to many difficult post-flop spots where we are either dominated or struggling to realize equity. Folding is the most straightforward and mathematically sound decision.

43o is a very weak unsuited hand. Even from the Button, it is not profitable to play, especially against an opponent who frequently 3-bets or defends the big blind aggressively. Folding to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with 6d 7d, a solid suited connector. In heads-up or short-handed play, this is a strong hand to open with. I will maintain my aggressive image by raising to 300, which puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the pot early.
93o is an extremely weak hand and highly unplayable, especially facing a raise from the button. Folding is the standard and only reasonable option here to preserve my stack.

With Ace-King suited (AKs) on the button in a heads-up match, I have a premium starting hand. Raising to 3 big blinds is a standard and strong open to build the pot, take control of the hand, and extract value from worse hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Pro has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. While the pot odds are 2:1 to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and want to maximize my long-term tournament standing, there is no reason to defend such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 7h 5c. This is a speculative hand that plays okay in position, but I have a significant stack lead over Pro. Given the recent history of Pro folding to my raises or the game being relatively tight, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to try and take the pot down immediately or play the hand in position.

Pocket fives are a strong starting hand in heads-up play, often ahead of the opponent's wide opening range. Facing a 3x raise, calling from the big blind is a standard and profitable play. While 3-betting is an option, it can bloat the pot out of position and force us to fold to a 4-bet. Calling allows us to see a flop, potentially hit a set, or navigate a favorable runout with a manageable pot size.

On this flop of Ts 8h 4h, our pocket fives are an underpair to the Ten and Eight. Since we called the pre-flop raise out of position, the standard approach is to check to the aggressor. This allows us to evaluate Flash's action and pot control with a marginal made hand.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board of Ts-8h-4h is somewhat coordinated but also offers me a gutshot straight draw with my 7h 5c. Since Pro checked to me, I will put in a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent a stronger hand and try to take the pot down immediately. If called, I still have the possibility of hitting a 6 on the turn to improve to a straight.

On a Ts 8h 4h flop, our pocket fives are a very marginal third pair. We are facing a continuation bet from Flash. While it's possible Flash is c-betting with air, this board connects well with many hands in their pre-flop raising range, such as Tx, 8x, flush draws, and straight draws (like J9 or 97). We are out of position, and many turn cards will be difficult to navigate or will significantly downgrade our hand's relative strength. Folding here preserves our stack for better opportunities.

74o is a very weak unsuited and disconnected hand. From the Small Blind (Button) in heads-up play, it lacks the equity and playability required to justify a limp or a raise. The optimal strategy is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on the button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My standard strategy throughout this tournament has been to open for 300 (3x the big blind) from the small blind/button. Pro has folded to this raise many times in the past few hands, but with a hand this strong, I am more than happy to be called or to see a 3-bet. I will stick to my consistent sizing to maintain balance while extracting value.
